event: The Last Judgment, when Christ enthroned in glory separates the righteous from the wicked at the end of time
symbols: Christ enthroned at the summit in radiant glory — the Son of Man coming to judge the living and the dead
the large wooden cross held aloft at center — the instrument of salvation displayed as the standard of judgment, the sign of the Son of Man appearing in heaven
Mary and John the Baptist flanking Christ in Deesis — the two principal intercessors pleading on behalf of trembling humanity
angels with trumpets summoning the dead — the great trumpet blast of the last day
separation of figures into heavenly realm above and earthly chaos below — the division of sheep from goats
naked figures at the bottom — the dead raised from graves, stripped of pretension before the all-seeing Judge
description: A monumental vertical composition depicting the Last Judgment. At the apex, Christ sits enthroned in golden light, flanked by the Virgin Mary and John the Baptist in the Deesis arrangement. Below, a large wooden cross is held upright by angelic figures. Rows of saints, apostles, and patriarchs in richly colored robes are arranged in concentric cloud-banks around the heavenly court. In the middle register, angels with outstretched wings act as agents of divine justice.
theological_description: Pacheco's Last Judgment represents the culminating event of salvation history when Christ returns in glory to render final judgment upon all humanity. Christ at the summit fulfills his own prophecy: 'When the Son of Man comes in his glory, and all the angels with him, then he will sit on his glorious throne. Before him will be gathered all the nations, and he will separate people one from another as a shepherd separates the sheep from the goats' (Matthew 25:31-32). The cross at center is not decorative but the criterion of judgment itself -- the standard by which all human history is evaluated. As Paul wrote, 'the word of the cross is folly to those who are perishing, but to us who are being saved it is the power of God' (1 Corinthians 1:18). The Deesis composition expresses the conviction that even at judgment, intercession continues. The saints arrayed in glory participate in the judicial act: 'Do you not know that the saints will judge the world?' (1 Corinthians 6:2). The naked rising figures embody bodily resurrection: 'we must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ, so that each one may receive what is due for what he has done in the body' (2 Corinthians 5:10).

scripture_refs.reason: The Son of Man on his throne separating sheep from goats -- the central biblical text for the Last Judgment
The great white throne judgment and the dead judged according to their deeds
The sign of the Son of Man in heaven and angels gathering the elect with a trumpet -- the cross and angelic trumpeters depicted
We must all appear before the judgment seat of Christ -- the risen figures standing before the Judge
The word of the cross is the power of God -- the cross held aloft as the standard of judgment
The Ancient of Days took his seat; the court sat in judgment and the books were opened -- Old Testament vision of the heavenly tribunal
All in the tombs will hear his voice and come out to resurrection of life or judgment
The saints will judge the world -- saints arrayed around the throne as participants in judgment
